title: Getting started
heading: Getting started
text: Install the CLI, pick a platform, install just that toolchain, run doctor, then log in only to publish. New Soroq users macOS or Linux Only needed to publish

title: Getting started
heading: Before you begin
text: Before you begin Soroq delivers hard over-the-air patches to Flutter apps: cut a base release, ship a code (Android) or engine (iOS) patch, watch it stage and activate, and roll back on demand. Installs and doctor work with no account — only publishing needs a login. Experimental Both platforms are experimental. Android is fresh-user proven (experimental (fresh-user proven)). iOS is experimental, device-only (physical iPhone, fresh-user proven). macOS and Linux are supported; Windows is pending.

title: Getting started
heading: 1. Install the Soroq CLI
text: 1. Install the Soroq CLI Run the public installer, add the bin directory to your PATH, and confirm the version. This installs both soroq and soroqctl. No login required. soroq v0.2.2 Choose your platform and install its toolchain. curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 https://raw.githubusercontent.com/soroq/install/main/install.sh -sSf | bash export PATH="$HOME/.soroq/bin:$PATH" soroq version # -> soroq v0.2.2 Prefer to build it yourself? Clone github.com/soroq/install and run make build in backend/. Full install, checksum verification, and quarantine steps are on the CLI page.

title: Getting started
heading: 2. Choose a platform and install its toolchain
text: 2. Choose a platform and install its toolchain Pick Android or iOS with the selector above, then pin the frontend and only that platform's toolchain. You do not install both. Finish with doctor. None of these steps require a login. toolchain doctor: frontend + toolchain present and consistent Log in — but only when you are ready to publish. soroq frontend install soroq-flutter-frontend-f74781f6-6903c161 --api https://api.soroq.dev soroq toolchain install soroq-android-3.44.2-release-12d3315131f5 --api https://api.soroq.dev soroq toolchain doctor soroq frontend install soroq-flutter-frontend-f74781f6-6903c161 --api https://api.soroq.dev soroq toolchain install soroq-ios-3.44.2-profile-f74781f6-3499c008-local-r3-dynmodules --api https://api.soroq.dev soroq toolchain doctor soroq toolchain doctor reports whether the frontend and the toolchain you installed are present and consistent before you build anything. Download size The frontend archive is about 1.06 GiB (1,140,170,246 bytes, measured from its /archive endpoint). Toolchain archive sizes are not published (their /archive HEAD is not served over the API), so expect an additional download on first install. Have a solid connection and some disk headroom.

title: Getting started
heading: 3. Log in (only to publish)
text: 3. Log in (only to publish) Authenticate against the hosted surface, then confirm your identity. You only need this immediately before you publish a release or a patch — installs and doctor never ask for it. logged in as <your-operator-email> Follow the Android or iOS quickstart for a full base -> patch -> rollback cycle. soroq login --hosted-surface https://soroq.dev --api https://api.soroq.dev soroq whoami --api https://api.soroq.dev

title: Getting started
heading: 4. Ship your first patch
text: 4. Ship your first patch Everything is installed. Follow the platform quickstart to run a complete cycle: cut a base release, publish a visible patch at full rollout, verify it, then roll back.

title: Android quickstart
heading: Android quickstart
text: Take a stock Flutter APK to a signed code patch at full rollout, verify it, then roll it back. Android experimental (fresh-user proven) base -> patch -> rollback

title: Android quickstart
heading: Set up your identifiers
text: Set up your identifiers Export these once so every command below reads them instead of a bare <id>. Each line says where its value comes from — you pick most of them; Soroq returns status under the ids you provide. Create the app and register Soroq. SOROQ_API The Soroq control-plane API. Always this value for the hosted service. SOROQ_APP_ID A stable identifier you pick for the app. Reuse it across every release and patch. SOROQ_CHANNEL The release channel devices subscribe to, e.g. stable or beta. SOROQ_RELEASE_ID A label for the base release this patch targets. Soroq records it under this id. SOROQ_PATCH_ID A label you pick for this patch. Soroq returns its rollout + verification status under this id.

title: Android quickstart
heading: 1. Create the app and add Soroq
text: 1. Create the app and add Soroq Scaffold a stock Flutter app, add the runtime package, and initialize Soroq with the identifiers you exported. soroq.yaml written; app registered on the channel Install the frontend and the Android toolchain. flutter create my_app cd my_app flutter pub add soroq_flutter # soroq_flutter 0.2.3 soroq init --app-id "$SOROQ_APP_ID" --channel "$SOROQ_CHANNEL" --api "$SOROQ_API"

title: Android quickstart
heading: 2. Install the frontend and Android toolchain
text: 2. Install the frontend and Android toolchain Pin the shared frontend and the Android toolchain, then run doctor. These are downloads, not a login — no account is needed yet. Skip this only if doctor already reports both present. toolchain doctor: frontend + Android toolchain present and consistent Cut the base release. soroq frontend install soroq-flutter-frontend-f74781f6-6903c161 --api "$SOROQ_API" soroq toolchain install soroq-android-3.44.2-release-12d3315131f5 --api "$SOROQ_API" soroq toolchain doctor Download size The frontend archive is about 1.06 GiB (1,140,170,246 bytes), installed under ~/.soroq/frontends/ (toolchains under ~/.soroq/toolchains/). Expect an additional toolchain download on first install, so keep some disk headroom. A verified existing install is reused; re-run with --force to reinstall.

title: Android quickstart
heading: 3. Cut the base release
text: 3. Cut the base release Publishing needs a one-time browser login (installs and doctor did not). Then register the stock APK as the base the patch will target — the stock app shows the default counter value (42). Run this from inside my_app. logged in; base release accepted for $SOROQ_RELEASE_ID (version 1.0.0+1) Change visible code and publish a patch. soroq login --api "$SOROQ_API" # one-time: opens the browser to sign in soroq release android \ --toolchain soroq-android-3.44.2-release-12d3315131f5 \ --artifact-type apk --api "$SOROQ_API" \ --app-id "$SOROQ_APP_ID" --release-id "$SOROQ_RELEASE_ID" \ --version 1.0.0+1 --channel "$SOROQ_CHANNEL"

title: Android quickstart
heading: 4. Change visible code and patch
text: 4. Change visible code and patch Edit a lib/ Dart file so a visible value changes (for example a counter that reads 42 -> 91), then publish a code patch at 100% rollout. patch $SOROQ_PATCH_ID published at 100% rollout Confirm the patch staged and activated on device. soroq patch android \ --release-id "$SOROQ_RELEASE_ID" --patch-id "$SOROQ_PATCH_ID" \ --toolchain soroq-android-3.44.2-release-12d3315131f5 \ --artifact-type apk --api "$SOROQ_API" \ --channel "$SOROQ_CHANNEL" --track "$SOROQ_CHANNEL" \ --kind code --rollout 100 Two-cold-start model The first launch after a patch is published stages it (downloads + verifies). The NEXT cold start activates it. A single launch never both stages and activates.

title: Android quickstart
heading: 5. Confirm the patch status on device
text: 5. Confirm the patch status on device Cold-start the app once to stage the patch, then cold-start again to activate it. The visible value flips from 42 to 91 on the second launch. In code, read getAutoUpdateState() after the check completes (not the immediate return of runAutoUpdateNow()) to confirm the patch is staged then active before you move on. auto-update state: patch $SOROQ_PATCH_ID active; app shows value 91 Roll back to the base. Once the second cold start shows the patched value, the patch is live at 100% rollout on the channel. That on-device value is the ground-truth status.

title: Android quickstart
heading: 6. Roll back
text: 6. Roll back Revert to the base with a server-side rollback and verify it landed. rollback verified for $SOROQ_PATCH_ID soroq rollback --patch-id "$SOROQ_PATCH_ID" --api "$SOROQ_API" --verify Rollback nuance An already-running process may still show patched code for that launch. The NEXT cold start serves the base. Rollback is a server-side decision; --verify confirms it landed.

title: Android quickstart
heading: Proven flow
text: Proven flow This exact cycle has been exercised end to end on the experimental hard-OTA tier. Base app shows value 42 Patch app shows value 91 after activation Rollback next cold start returns to 42 Tamper a tampered patch is refused (fail-closed)

title: iOS quickstart
heading: iOS quickstart
text: Patch a running Flutter engine on a physical iPhone, then roll back. Device-only, signing required. iOS experimental, device-only (physical iPhone, fresh-user proven) Physical iPhone + Apple signing

title: iOS quickstart
heading: Requirements
text: Requirements iOS hard OTA is experimental and runs on a physical device only. It does not run on the simulator, and Apple signing is required to install and run on device. Physical iPhone only The simulator is not supported. You need a real device, Apple signing set up, and the experimental iOS engine/toolchain tier.

title: iOS quickstart
heading: Set up your identifiers
text: Set up your identifiers Export these once so no command carries a bare <id>. The manifest signing keypair is generated for you — you never export the private seed. Create the app and install the frontend + iOS toolchain. SOROQ_API The Soroq control-plane API. Always this value for the hosted service. SOROQ_APP_ID A stable identifier you pick for the app. Reuse it across releases and patches. SOROQ_CHANNEL The release channel devices subscribe to, e.g. stable or beta. SOROQ_RELEASE_ID A label for the base engine release this patch targets. Soroq records it under this id. SOROQ_PATCH_ID A label you pick for this engine patch. Soroq returns its status under this id. manifest_trust is generated Soroq scaffolds manifest_trust for you: only the public key is written into your project. The private seed is stored at mode 0600 and gitignored — it is generated, never something you set.

title: iOS quickstart
heading: 1. Create the app, init Soroq, install frontend + toolchain
text: 1. Create the app, init Soroq, install frontend + toolchain Scaffold a stock Flutter app, add the runtime package, and run soroq init. init writes soroq.yaml and scaffolds manifest_trust (public key in the project, private seed at mode 0600, gitignored) — so the file you edit in the next step already exists and the base you build in step 3 has a key to sign with. soroq.yaml written + manifest_trust scaffolded; frontend + iOS toolchain installed Declare the functions the engine may patch. flutter create my_app && cd my_app flutter pub add soroq_flutter # soroq_flutter 0.2.3 soroq init --app-id "$SOROQ_APP_ID" --channel "$SOROQ_CHANNEL" --api "$SOROQ_API" soroq frontend install soroq-flutter-frontend-f74781f6-6903c161 --api "$SOROQ_API" soroq toolchain install soroq-ios-3.44.2-profile-f74781f6-3499c008-local-r3-dynmodules --api "$SOROQ_API"

title: iOS quickstart
heading: 2. Declare patchable functions in soroq.yaml
text: 2. Declare patchable functions in soroq.yaml List the functions the engine is allowed to patch under ios_engine, using the lib/<file>.dart#<function> form. Build the signed base release. ios_engine: enabled: true patchable: - "lib/foo.dart#myFn"

title: iOS quickstart
heading: 3. Build the signed base release
text: 3. Build the signed base release Publishing needs a one-time browser login (installs and doctor did not). Then build and register the signed base engine release. logged in; signed base engine release built for $SOROQ_RELEASE_ID Publish an engine patch. soroq login --api "$SOROQ_API" # one-time: opens the browser to sign in soroq release ios --engine --build \ --toolchain soroq-ios-3.44.2-profile-f74781f6-3499c008-local-r3-dynmodules \ --app-id "$SOROQ_APP_ID" --release-id "$SOROQ_RELEASE_ID" \ --channel "$SOROQ_CHANNEL" --api "$SOROQ_API"

title: iOS quickstart
heading: 4. Publish an engine patch
text: 4. Publish an engine patch engine patch $SOROQ_PATCH_ID published Confirm the patched value on device, then roll back. soroq patch ios --engine \ --toolchain soroq-ios-3.44.2-profile-f74781f6-3499c008-local-r3-dynmodules \ --app-id "$SOROQ_APP_ID" --release-id "$SOROQ_RELEASE_ID" \ --patch-id "$SOROQ_PATCH_ID" \ --channel "$SOROQ_CHANNEL" --api "$SOROQ_API" Relaunch to activate, then read the status Cold-start the app on the iPhone to stage the engine patch, then cold-start again to activate it: the device value flips from the base to the patched value. That on-device value is the ground-truth status before you roll back. A patch may only direct-call retained or manifest-listed symbols. Calls into symbols that were stripped are not available to the patch.

title: iOS quickstart
heading: 5. Roll back
text: 5. Roll back rollback verified for $SOROQ_PATCH_ID soroq rollback ios-engine --patch-id "$SOROQ_PATCH_ID" --api "$SOROQ_API" --verify Rollback nuance An app already running with the patch may still show the patched value for that launch. The NEXT cold start serves the base value — cold-start the iPhone again to confirm it returned to base.

title: iOS quickstart
heading: Expected values
text: Expected values Base device shows the base value Patch device shows the patched value Rollback device returns to the base value Tamper refused: sig=FAIL, bad manifest signature (fail-closed)

title: CLI reference
heading: CLI reference
text: Install on macOS or Linux, build from source, verify the download, and run soroq + soroqctl. v0.2.2 macOS + Linux (Windows pending) soroq, soroqctl

title: CLI reference
heading: Install on macOS or Linux
text: Install on macOS or Linux Run the public installer, add the bin directory to your PATH, and check the version. install.sh auto-detects OS + architecture; macOS (Apple Silicon + Intel) and Linux (amd64 + arm64) are supported. soroq v0.2.2 curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 https://raw.githubusercontent.com/soroq/install/main/install.sh -sSf | bash export PATH="$HOME/.soroq/bin:$PATH" soroq version # -> soroq v0.2.2 macOS and Linux are supported and smoke-tested — Linux is natively validated in CI, not under emulation. Windows is pending (see the Windows acceptance checklist). Building from source is supported on all three.

title: CLI reference
heading: Build from source
text: Build from source backend/ is the public CLI source — the same client code shipped in the binary releases, exported deterministically (operator-only publishing and private control-plane code are excluded). No private module, private Git dependency, or local path is required. soroq v0.2.2 git clone https://github.com/soroq/install cd install/backend make build # stamps ./VERSION -> ./soroq + ./soroqctl ./soroq version # -> soroq v0.2.2 # or plainly, without the Makefile: go build ./cmd/soroq ./cmd/soroqctl go test ./... The two operator-only commands (frontend publish, toolchain publish) are intentionally not in this build; every normal developer command (install/doctor, login/whoami/logout, init, release, patch, rollback) is present.

title: CLI reference
heading: Verify the download (SHA256)
text: Verify the download (SHA256) install.sh verifies the SHA256 automatically. To check manually, download the release tarball and checksums.txt from the public release, then verify. soroq: OK shasum -a 256 -c checksums.txt

title: CLI reference
heading: Clear the macOS quarantine
text: Clear the macOS quarantine If Gatekeeper blocks the binaries, remove the quarantine attribute, then re-run the version check. soroq v0.2.2 xattr -dr com.apple.quarantine "$HOME/.soroq/bin/soroq" "$HOME/.soroq/bin/soroqctl" soroq version # -> soroq v0.2.2

title: Troubleshooting
heading: Troubleshooting
text: Fix the errors you are most likely to hit: install, login, stale status, and fail-closed signatures. Install, login, platforms, versions, signatures

title: Troubleshooting
heading: Login and identity
text: Login and identity Keychain not found On a fresh or sandboxed HOME the token falls back to ~/.soroq/config.json at mode 0600. Verify with soroq whoami.

title: Troubleshooting
heading: Missing frontend or toolchain
text: Missing frontend or toolchain Frontend missing soroq frontend install soroq-flutter-frontend-f74781f6-6903c161 --api https://api.soroq.dev Toolchain missing soroq toolchain install <toolchain> --api https://api.soroq.dev (Android: soroq-android-3.44.2-release-12d3315131f5, iOS: soroq-ios-3.44.2-profile-f74781f6-3499c008-local-r3-dynmodules)

title: Troubleshooting
heading: Android
text: Android Status looks stale after a check Read getAutoUpdateState() after the check completes, not only the immediate return value of runAutoUpdateNow(). Rollback still shows patched code An already-running process may keep patched code for that launch. The NEXT cold start serves the base.

title: Troubleshooting
heading: iOS
text: iOS Nothing happens on the simulator Hard OTA is physical-device only. The simulator is not supported. App will not install or run Apple signing is required to install and run on device.

title: Troubleshooting
heading: Platforms
text: Platforms Windows Windows is pending. install.sh does not offer it and install.ps1 stays gated behind SOROQ_INSTALL_ALLOW_WINDOWS=1. It becomes supported only after the interactive gates pass; see the Windows acceptance checklist. Prefer building from source Supported on macOS and Linux (Windows pending): clone https://github.com/soroq/install, cd install/backend, make build (or go build ./cmd/soroq ./cmd/soroqctl) -> soroq v0.2.2.

title: Troubleshooting
heading: Versions and signatures
text: Versions and signatures Version bump / runtime_id mismatch Patches are version and runtime specific. A version bump needs a new base release and a new patch built against it. manifest_signature_invalid / tamper error This is fail-closed: the patch is NOT applied. Rebuild and re-sign from a trusted manifest.

title: What is Soroq
heading: What is Soroq
text: A release-control layer that delivers hard over-the-air patches to eligible Flutter code, assets, and the iOS engine — not native or store-level changes. Concept Eligible Flutter code/assets + iOS engine Experimental

title: What is Soroq
heading: What Soroq is
text: What Soroq is Soroq is a release-control layer for Flutter apps. You cut a signed base release, ship a hard over-the-air patch against it, watch the patch stage and activate on a cold start, and roll back on demand. On Android it delivers a code patch; on iOS it patches the running Flutter engine. Installs and doctor need no account — only publishing a release or patch requires a login. Experimental on both platforms Android is experimental (fresh-user proven). iOS is experimental, device-only (physical iPhone, fresh-user proven). macOS and Linux are supported for the CLI; Windows is pending.

title: What is Soroq
heading: What it updates
text: What it updates A patch replaces eligible application content built against a specific base release and runtime. Android Eligible Flutter/Dart code and bundled assets, delivered as a code patch against a base APK release. iOS The running Flutter engine: functions you declare as patchable under ios_engine, against a signed base engine release. Always signed Every patch is delivered under a signed manifest and is version- and runtime-specific to the base it targets.

title: What is Soroq
heading: What it does not touch
text: What it does not touch Soroq does not replace the platform release process. It does not update native (Kotlin/Swift/Objective-C) code, embedded native libraries, app entitlements, or anything that requires a new store submission. It is not a store-distribution channel. A version bump or any native change still needs a fresh base release cut with the toolchain, and a new patch built against that base.

title: Before you begin
heading: Before you begin
text: Prerequisites: Flutter, a pub.dev-published app, macOS or Linux for the CLI, and — for iOS — a physical iPhone with Apple signing. macOS or Linux (Windows pending) Physical iPhone + macOS/Xcode + Apple signing ~1.06 GiB for the frontend

title: Before you begin
heading: For every platform
text: For every platform You need a working Flutter toolchain, a Flutter app that pulls in soroq_flutter from pub.dev, and a machine that can run the Soroq CLI. Flutter A current stable Flutter SDK on your PATH (flutter --version works). A pub.dev app A Flutter app that depends on soroq_flutter (0.2.3) from pub.dev — add it with flutter pub add soroq_flutter. CLI host macOS or Linux to run the soroq CLI. Windows is pending; building from source is supported on macOS and Linux. Disk Room for the shared frontend archive: about 1.06 GiB (1,140,170,246 bytes), plus a per-platform toolchain download.

title: Before you begin
heading: Additional requirements for iOS
text: Additional requirements for iOS iOS hard OTA is experimental and physical-device only. It does not run on the simulator. iOS is device-only You need a physical iPhone, macOS with Xcode, and Apple code signing set up to install and run on device. The simulator is not supported.

title: Installation
heading: Installation
text: Install the CLI, pin the shared frontend, add the platform toolchain you need, or build the CLI from source. Windows is pending. v0.2.2 ~1.06 GiB archive macOS + Linux (Windows pending)

title: Installation
heading: 1. Install the CLI
text: 1. Install the CLI Run the public installer, add the bin directory to your PATH, and confirm the version. This installs both soroq and soroqctl. No login required. soroq v0.2.2 Install the shared frontend. curl --proto '=https' --tlsv1.2 https://raw.githubusercontent.com/soroq/install/main/install.sh -sSf | bash export PATH="$HOME/.soroq/bin:$PATH" soroq version # -> soroq v0.2.2

title: Installation
heading: 2. Install the frontend
text: 2. Install the frontend Pin the shared Flutter frontend both platforms build against. This is a one-time download of about 1.06 GiB. frontend installed Install the toolchain for your platform. soroq frontend install soroq-flutter-frontend-f74781f6-6903c161 --api https://api.soroq.dev Download size The frontend archive is about 1.06 GiB (1,140,170,246 bytes, measured from its /archive endpoint). Toolchain archive sizes are not published, so expect an extra download on first toolchain install.

title: Installation
heading: 3. Install a platform toolchain
text: 3. Install a platform toolchain Install only the toolchain for the platform you are shipping, then run doctor to confirm the frontend and toolchain are consistent. toolchain doctor: frontend + toolchain present and consistent Optionally build the CLI from source. soroq toolchain install soroq-android-3.44.2-release-12d3315131f5 --api https://api.soroq.dev soroq toolchain doctor soroq toolchain install soroq-ios-3.44.2-profile-f74781f6-3499c008-local-r3-dynmodules --api https://api.soroq.dev soroq toolchain doctor

title: Installation
heading: Build from source
text: Build from source backend/ is the public CLI source. Clone it and build — no private module, private Git dependency, or local path is required. soroq v0.2.2 git clone https://github.com/soroq/install cd install/backend make build # stamps ./VERSION -> ./soroq + ./soroqctl ./soroq version # -> soroq v0.2.2 Windows is pending install.sh does not offer Windows and install.ps1 stays gated behind an explicit opt-in. Building from source is supported on macOS and Linux; Windows becomes supported only after the acceptance gates pass.

title: Authentication
heading: Authentication
text: Log in through the browser only when you are ready to publish. Tokens live in the OS keychain, or a mode-0600 file when no keychain is available. Only to publish a release or patch login, whoami, logout Keychain or ~/.soroq/config.json (0600)

title: Authentication
heading: When you need to log in
text: When you need to log in Installs and doctor never ask for an account. You only authenticate immediately before you publish a release or a patch. Logging in opens the hosted surface in your browser and returns an operator identity to the CLI. Browser login is the primary path. Do not wire a raw operator token into your environment as the normal way to authenticate — use soroq login.

title: Authentication
heading: Log in, check identity, log out
text: Log in, check identity, log out logged in as <your-operator-email> soroq login --hosted-surface https://soroq.dev --api https://api.soroq.dev soroq whoami --api https://api.soroq.dev soroq logout

title: Authentication
heading: Where the token is stored
text: Where the token is stored The CLI prefers the operating system keychain. On a fresh or sandboxed HOME where no keychain is available, it falls back to a plain file at mode 0600. Keychain The default and preferred store when the OS keychain is reachable. 0600 file fallback ~/.soroq/config.json at mode 0600 when the keychain is unavailable. Verify either way with soroq whoami.

title: soroq.yaml reference
heading: soroq.yaml reference
text: The keys soroq init writes and the ones you edit: app_id, channel, the ios_engine block, and the auto-scaffolded manifest_trust. soroq init app_id, channel, ios_engine, manifest_trust Project root

title: soroq.yaml reference
heading: Keys
text: Keys soroq init writes soroq.yaml at your project root. These are the keys it uses. app_id The stable identifier you pick for the app, reused across every release and patch (e.g. com.example.my_app). channel The release channel devices subscribe to, e.g. stable or beta. ios_engine.enabled Set true to enable iOS engine patching for this app. ios_engine.patchable The list of functions the engine may patch, each in lib/<file>.dart#<function> form. manifest_trust Auto-scaffolded signing trust. soroq writes only the public key here; the private seed is stored separately at mode 0600 and gitignored.

title: soroq.yaml reference
heading: Example
text: Example A minimal soroq.yaml for an app that ships iOS engine patches. manifest_trust is generated for you — you never paste in a private seed. app_id: "com.example.my_app" channel: "stable" ios_engine: enabled: true patchable: - "lib/foo.dart#myFn" manifest_trust: public_key: "<auto-scaffolded public key>" manifest_trust is generated You do not set manifest_trust by hand. Soroq scaffolds it: only the public key lands in the project; the private seed stays at mode 0600 and gitignored.

title: Compatibility & limitations
heading: Compatibility & limitations
text: What is eligible for a patch versus what is blocked, the experimental boundaries, and the platform support matrix. macOS + Linux (Windows pending) OTA experimental OTA experimental, device-only

title: Compatibility & limitations
heading: Eligible vs blocked
text: Eligible vs blocked A patch may only change content built against the base release it targets. Eligible Android: eligible Flutter/Dart code and bundled assets. iOS: engine functions you declare under ios_engine.patchable. Blocked Native code (Kotlin/Swift/Objective-C), embedded native libraries, entitlements, and anything that needs a new store submission. iOS symbol rule An iOS engine patch may only direct-call retained or manifest-listed symbols; calls into stripped (tree-shaken) symbols are not available to the patch. Version-specific Patches are version- and runtime-specific. A version bump needs a new base release and a new patch built against it.

title: Compatibility & limitations
heading: Platform support matrix
text: Platform support matrix CLI: macOS Supported. CLI: Linux Supported (natively validated in CI, not emulated). CLI: Windows Pending — gated behind an explicit opt-in until the acceptance gates pass. Android OTA experimental (fresh-user proven). iOS OTA experimental, device-only (physical iPhone, fresh-user proven). The simulator is not supported.

title: Compatibility & limitations
heading: Experimental boundaries
text: Experimental boundaries Both platforms are experimental Treat hard OTA as an experimental capability. iOS runs on a physical device only, and Apple signing is required to install and run on device.

title: Security model
heading: Security model
text: Every patch ships under a signed manifest verified against a pinned Ed25519 key. A bad signature is refused fail-closed, patches must match their base, and rollback is always available. Ed25519 signed manifests Fail-closed on bad signature Server-side rollback

title: Security model
heading: Signed manifests and Ed25519 trust
text: Signed manifests and Ed25519 trust Every release and patch is delivered under a manifest signed with an Ed25519 keypair. The public key is scaffolded into your project as manifest_trust; the private seed is stored at mode 0600 and gitignored. The device verifies the manifest signature against the pinned public key before it will apply anything.

title: Security model
heading: Fail-closed on a bad signature
text: Fail-closed on a bad signature If the manifest signature does not verify, the patch is refused and NOT applied. There is no soft-fail path. Tamper is refused A tampered patch fails verification (sig=FAIL) and is rejected fail-closed. The app keeps running its current trusted code; rebuild and re-sign from a trusted manifest to recover.

title: Security model
heading: Base-match and rollback
text: Base-match and rollback Base match A patch is version- and runtime-specific and only applies against the exact base release it was built for. A mismatch is not applied. Rollback Rollback is a server-side decision; the next cold start serves the base. Use --verify to confirm it landed.

title: Product status
heading: Product status
text: The honest state of Soroq: CLI on macOS and Linux, Android fresh-user proven, iOS fresh-user proven on a physical device, all experimental. v0.2.2 experimental (fresh-user proven) experimental, device-only (physical iPhone, fresh-user proven)

title: Product status
heading: Where each piece stands
text: Where each piece stands CLI soroq v0.2.2 on macOS and Linux. Windows is pending. Building from source is supported on macOS and Linux. Android OTA experimental (fresh-user proven): the base -> patch -> rollback cycle has been exercised on emulator and device. iOS OTA experimental, device-only (physical iPhone, fresh-user proven): fresh-user proven on a physical iPhone, including a TestFlight install. The simulator is not supported. Packages soroq_flutter 0.2.3, soroq_sdk 0.1.6, from pub.dev.

title: Product status
heading: What we do not claim
text: What we do not claim Soroq does not claim App-Store or Play-Store production readiness, and it does not promise to hot-reload every kind of change. Both platforms are experimental. Experimental, honestly scoped Hard OTA on Android and iOS is experimental. iOS is device-only and requires Apple signing. Scope your expectations to the proven base -> patch -> rollback cycle.
